kartikeya-hridayam: Skanda, son of Shiva and Parvati, leads the armies of the gods into battle — and this short hymn distills his power into its heart-essence form. कार्तिकेय हृदयम् runs to just 7 verses, about 4 minutes of recitation, yet tradition holds that it dissolves enemies and obstacles through the raw shakti it carries. Reciters call on Kartikeya here not only as a warrior but as Jnana-Shakti and Guru, the giver of courage, leadership, and clarity of purpose. The Skanda Purana preserves it. Tuesdays suit it best, and devotees lean on it heavily during Skanda Shashthi, when the commander's grace is felt most keenly.
kartikeya-sahasranama: A thousand names of Skanda — also worshipped as Murugan — gathered into 25 verses that take roughly 22 minutes to chant in full. The Skanda Purana frames this litany as a complete sadhana: parents pray it for the blessing of sons, students for learning, and those weighed down by hardship recite it to be rid of sins, diseases, and sorrows, with access to Skanda loka promised to the steady devotee. Children are guarded against bala graha, the afflictions of early childhood. Seekers of prosperity find wealth named here; seekers of release find moksha. कार्तिकेय सहस्रनाम स्तोत्रम् belongs to Tuesdays and Thursdays, and rises in power across Skanda Shashthi, Kartik Purnima, and Thaipusam.
kartikeya-stotram: When litigation drags on, when rivals close in, when a battle of any kind has to be won, devotees have long turned to this hymn of the warrior-god. कार्तिकेय स्तोत्रम् gathers the major names and forms of Kartikeya across its 10 verses, invoking him as commander of the celestial army and summoning courage, valor, and victory in every contest of life. Strangely for a martial prayer, it closes by asking for sham — peace — through Skanda's blessing, balancing the sword with calm. The Skanda Purana is its home. About 6 minutes of recitation suits a Tuesday, with Skanda Shashti and Kartik Purnima the high points of the year for it.
karva-chauth-stotram: On Kartik Krishna Chaturthi, married women keep a fast for their husbands' long life and break it only at the sight of the moon — and this prayer accompanies that vow. करवा चौथ स्तोत्रम् weaves Sanskrit shlokas together with the Hindi prayers women have spoken for generations, calling on Gauri and Shankar, the divine couple, for harmony in the home. Its central plea is akhand saubhagya: unbroken marital bliss carried across seven births. The 10 verses take about 6 minutes. Drawn from the Karva Chauth Vrat Katha tradition, it sits naturally on Mondays and Fridays through the year, but finds its true moment on the night of Karva Chauth itself, recited as the moon clears the horizon.
karva-chauth-vrat-katha: Few observances hold the place that Karva Chauth does among married women of North India — a nirjala fast kept from sunrise to moonrise, neither food nor water, offered as the plainest possible proof of love for a husband. Reading this katha is not optional to the vrat; it is the vrat vidhi itself, and the 20 verses (about 10 minutes) carry the story that gives the fast its meaning. करवा चौथ व्रत कथा draws on the Puranic vrat-katha tradition and calls on Shiva and Parvati, the ideal couple, to bless the marriage and lengthen the husband's years. It belongs to the month of Kartik, kept with great feeling across the northern plains.
kashi-vishwanath-stotram: To chant these verses, the Kashi Khanda of the Skanda Purana promises, is to worship Vishwanath in Kashi itself — the great Shiva lingam by the Ganga, without the journey. श्री काशी विश्वनाथ स्तोत्रम् runs to 11 verses, around 10 minutes of recitation, and asks for knowledge, prosperity, happiness, and fame while burning away sin the way fire clears a forest. The deeper promise is twofold: Shiva is said to whisper the Taraka Brahma mantra into the dying devotee's ear, and the soul attains shiva-sayujya, union with the Lord. Mondays suit it, as do Maha Shivaratri and the Mondays of Shravan.
kathopanishad-key-verses: Nachiketa, a boy, stands before Yama, lord of death, and refuses every bribe until he is taught the one thing worth knowing — what survives the body. From that dialogue come the key verses gathered here: Uttishthata Jagrata, the call to arise and awake; the body imagined as a chariot with the atman as its rider; the hard choice between shreya, the good, and preya, the merely pleasant. कठोपनिषद् (प्रमुख श्लोक) reveals the Self as unborn and imperishable and points the way to amritatvam, deathlessness. Fifteen verses, about 12 minutes. Drawn from the Kathopanishad of the Krishna Yajurveda, it rewards study on Thursdays, Saturdays, and especially Guru Purnima.
kedarnath-stotram: High in the Garhwal Himalayas stands the Kedarnath shrine, and the Shiva Purana holds that reciting this hymn earns the same merit as the arduous climb to reach it. श्री केदारनाथ स्तोत्रम् runs 11 verses, roughly 8 minutes, and is sung to clear away sins and negative karma and to bring the devotee to shiva-sayujya, union with the Lord. Recited at the three sandhya junctions of the day, it is said to fulfill heartfelt desires; recited in danger, it shields against calamity. Alongside Shiva it honors Parvati, Ganesha, and Kartikeya. Mondays are its day, and Maha Shivaratri and the Mondays of Shravan its season — a pilgrimage in verse for those who cannot make the trek.
kena-upanishad: It opens with a question no instrument can answer: by whose will does the mind think, the eye see, the breath move? केनोपनिषद्, from the Talavakara Brahmana of the Samaveda, pursues that inquiry into the ground of consciousness and arrives at a paradox — Brahman is the seer unseen, the hearer unheard, the knower no one knows as an object. Its Pratibodha Viditam verse locates that reality not in some distant heaven but in every flash of awareness. Fourteen verses, about 15 minutes of reading. One of the principal Upanishads, taken up by Shankaracharya in his commentaries, it points the steady seeker toward amritatvam — immortality through self-knowledge rather than ritual.
ketu-dosh-nivaran-stotram: Ketu, the shadow planet, scatters the mind — its mahadasha can bring confusion, aimlessness, a sense of drifting without anchor. This stotra answers that affliction by turning to Ganesha as the remover of Ketu-dosha. केतु दोष निवारण स्तोत्रम् does not merely suppress the planet's pull; it redirects its energy toward moksha and inner growth, treating Ketu less as an enemy than as a force to be steered. Seven verses, about 4 minutes. The graha-shanti tradition prescribes it on Tuesdays, recited seven times over, for those carrying Ketu's weight in their chart and seeking both astrological relief and a clearer spiritual direction.
kilak-stotram: The Durga Saptashati holds enormous power, but the Markandeya Purana set a lock — a kilak — over its mantras, and this short hymn is the key that turns it. कीलक स्तोत्रम्, just 7 verses and about 5 minutes, is recited so that the seven hundred verses of the Saptashati release their full force; without it, the siddhi of that recitation stays sealed. On its own it calls Durga's protection against demoniac forces and obstacles, and devotees reach for it when crisis demands divine intervention. Tradition even holds that it can grant spiritual accomplishment without further ritual. Tuesdays and Fridays suit it, and during Navratri it becomes an essential part of the Saptashati path.
krishna-ashtakam: Eight verses, about 5 minutes — best taken at dawn, when the mind is still quiet enough to receive Krishna's vision. कृष्णाष्टकम् is recited above all for purification: it is said to wash away sins gathered across crores of lifetimes and to lift the karmic obstacles cluttering the seeker's path. What remains is a settled, clear-minded peace and the blessing of beholding the Lord. There is no single classical source claimed for it; it lives in the wider Sanskrit stotra tradition. Devotees keep it on Wednesdays and bring it to the fore at Janmashtami, when Krishna's birth is celebrated and the heart turns most readily toward him.
krishna-ashtottara-shatanamavali: One hundred and eight names, each a doorway into a different facet of Krishna — and reciting them, the Bhagavatam and Vishnu Purana traditions hold, draws down divine love and devotion as surely as anything. कृष्ण अष्टोत्तर शतनामावली is the litany of choice for Janmashtami puja and for daily archana, offered name by name with flowers or kumkum. Beyond bhakti it is said to grant wisdom, prosperity, and every kind of happiness, washing away sins and opening the way to liberation through Krishna's grace. The full namavali takes around 15 minutes. Wednesdays carry it well, and its force peaks on Janmashtami, Ekadashi, Govardhan Puja, and Holi.
krishna-chalisa: Forty chaupais — chalis, forty, gives the form its name — framed by opening and closing dohas, bringing the recitation to 42 verse-units in all, about 15 minutes of singing. कृष्ण चालीसा praises Krishna alongside Radha in plain, heartfelt verse, the kind a household can recite together without a Sanskrit teacher present. Devotees turn to it to clear obstacles and sorrows, to draw peace and prosperity into the home, and to be shielded from harmful influences. Recited with faith, tradition says, it fulfills righteous desires and deepens the bond of bhakti. Wednesdays are its day, and Janmashtami its festival — a familiar, well-worn prayer for everyday Krishna worship.
krishna-dhyanam: This is a meditation collection, not a single hymn. A sequence carries the dhyana of Krishna from cradle to crown, infant to Dwarkadhish. Its most loved image is the Bal Mukunda shloka: the baby Krishna afloat on a vata leaf, sucking his toe upon the cosmic waters. कृष्ण ध्यानम् gathers that with the Govardhana leela, the Murlidhar form of the flute-player, and the Krishna bija mantra for deeper meditative practice, drawing on the Bhagavata Purana and the Gita Govinda. Eight verses, about four minutes. Short enough for daily dhyana on Wednesdays and through Janmashtami, Gopashtami, and Holi, when devotees dwell on each form in turn.
krishna-dwadash-nama: Twelve names, twelve aspects — from Hari at the start to Gopikapriya, the beloved of the gopis, at the close. कृष्ण द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् sets them in order so that a single name captures the warrior and the next the tender lover, the whole sweep of Krishna held in 12 short verses, about 4 minutes. The Bhagavata Purana and the Narada tradition give it a daily purpose: an easy recitation that wears away sins and, recited steadily, brings sarva kamartha siddhi, the fulfillment of every worthy desire. It honors Radha and Devaki beside Krishna. Keep it on Wednesdays, and let it ring at Janmashtami and Holi.
krishna-lila-stuti: Nine verses, and each one opens a chapter of Krishna's life — birth in the prison at Mathura, the pranks of Vrindavan, the lifting of Govardhan, the counsel on the field of Kurukshetra, the long arc that ends with the Mahabharata. श्री कृष्ण लीला स्तुति, compiled from the Bhagavata Purana, condenses that whole story into about 6 minutes of recitation, and tradition holds that reciting it yields the merit of reading the entire Purana. Dwelling on the lilas deepens devotion in a way bare praise cannot, and the steady devotee is led toward Krishna Sayujya, union with the Lord. Wednesdays suit it, with Janmashtami, Govardhan Puja, and Holi its festival peaks.
krishna-mangalashtakam: Mangala is the keynote here. That is why this hymn is chosen for new beginnings, weddings, and the opening of any venture under Krishna's blessing. कृष्ण मङ्गलाष्टकम् runs the full span of his life across eight verses, from the play of Vrindavan to the war at Kurukshetra, about five minutes in all. It honors Radha and Rukmini at his side, fills the heart with devotion and divine love, and promises liberation from sin together with a place in Goloka, his eternal abode. Rooted in the Bhagavata Purana tradition, it belongs to Wednesdays. And to the festivals of Krishna, Janmashtami above all, and Holi, whenever an auspicious note is wanted.
krishna-sahasranama: A thousand names of Krishna, gathered from the Mahabharata tradition, spanning every chapter of his life — the butter-thief of Gokula, the cowherd of Vrindavan, the prince of Mathura and Dvaraka, the charioteer at Kurukshetra. कृष्ण सहस्रनाम स्तोत्रम् runs 30 verses and takes a full half-hour to chant, no small commitment, and the reward it promises is large to match: the washing-away of all sins, the kindling of Krishna bhakti, the fulfillment of desire, and finally moksha. It invokes him as Jagadguru, the teacher of the world who spoke the Bhagavad Gita. Recited on Wednesdays — and on Janmashtami, Ekadashi, or Holi — its fruit is said to multiply many times over.
krishna-stuti: A devotee's plain prayer in 12 verses, about 8 minutes, calling on Krishna in all his forms alongside Radha. श्री कृष्ण स्तुति is recited to lift sorrows and afflictions from the heart and to purify the soul of accumulated sin, leaving peace, prosperity, and quiet happiness in their place. There is no single named scripture behind it; it belongs to the broad Sanskrit stotra tradition that everyday Krishna devotees have carried forward. Daily recitation suits it well — Wednesdays and Fridays especially — and it brightens at Janmashtami and Holi. Tradition adds a final grace: the steady reciter is led, at the end, to Vishnuloka.
kshamapana-stotram: No worship is performed without fault — a syllable misplaced, a step forgotten, an offering made carelessly — and this hymn exists precisely to close that gap. क्षमापन means the seeking of forgiveness, and श्री क्षमापन स्तोत्रम् is recited at the end of any puja or path to ask Vishnu's pardon for every offense, known and unknown, of body, speech, and mind. Across its 11 verses, about 7 minutes, the devotee surrenders all action to the Lord and, through honest repentance, is brought toward vishnu-sayujya. It completes a worship that errors might otherwise have spoiled. From Vaishnavite tradition, it suits Thursdays and Saturdays and is fitting on Ekadashi — the natural last word after any recitation.
kubera-ashtakam: Kubera, the divine treasurer and friend of Vishnu, keeps the nine treasures — the Navanidhis — and this eight-verse hymn asks him to open them to the devotee. कुबेर अष्टकम्, from the Skanda Purana, names wealth, grain, progeny, good fortune, and lasting prosperity among its gifts, and is recited to clear away poverty, misfortune, and financial suffering. Tradition speaks of prosperity beyond compare, carried not for one life but across every birth. Lakshmi stands beside Kubera in the worship. The recitation runs about 7 minutes. Thursdays and Fridays suit it, and it gathers real force on Dhanteras and Akshaya Tritiya, the days the household turns toward abundance.
kubera-chalisa: Built in the chalisa form — forty chaupais, chalis meaning forty, with framing dohas that bring it to 42 verse-units, about 15 minutes — this is a household prayer to the lord of wealth. कुबेर चालीसा asks Kubera to free the reciter from debts and loans, to steady Lakshmi within the home, and to open all nine kinds of divine treasure, the navnidhi. Beyond clearing poverty it is sung for success in business and advancement in one's work. Drawn from the traditional chalisa sangrah and honoring Lakshmi alongside Kubera, it belongs to Fridays and Thursdays, and grows especially potent on Dhanteras and Diwali — the nights when wealth is invited across the threshold.
kubera-gayatri: Short enough to fold into a japa session — 2 verses, barely 2 minutes — this is the Gayatri mantra of Kubera, the divine treasurer who guards the nine treasures, the nava-nidhi. कुबेर गायत्री is repeated to draw wealth, riches, and steady financial growth, and suits business owners and anyone working to build their fortunes as much as the household devotee. From the Kubera Tantra, and worshipped with Lakshmi nearby, it is chanted on Fridays and Thursdays, and most of all on Dhanteras and Diwali. Its brevity is the point: a mantra meant to be turned over again and again on the mala, day after day.
kubera-hridayam: The heart-essence of Kubera worship in 7 verses, about 4 minutes — and what it names is concrete. कुबेर हृदयम् lists all nine nidhis by name — Shankha, Padma, Mahapadma, Makara, and the rest — and asks specifically for wealth, gold, silver, gems, and property, not abstractions but the substance of prosperity. It is recited to break free of poverty and debt and to draw the treasurer's grace into one's circumstances. The Shiva Purana preserves it, and Lakshmi is honored alongside Kubera. Fridays and Tuesdays carry it, while Dhanteras and Diwali are its festivals — a focused, practical prayer for anyone set on lasting financial security.
kubera-kavacham: A kavacham is armor in the form of sound, and this one wraps the devotee in the protection of Kubera, treasurer of the gods. श्री कुबेर कवचम् shields every limb and every direction with a divine guard across its 7 verses, about 7 minutes, while invoking ashta aishwarya — the eight forms of divine wealth — and abundance of grain and prosperity besides. Tradition holds it can lift poverty permanently by Kubera's grace, joining defense to plenty in a single recitation. Its source is the Vishnu Dharmottara Purana. Chant it on Fridays and Thursdays, and bring it forward on Dhanteras, when the household seeks both security and the steady inflow of wealth.
kubera-lakshmi-stotram: Two powers of wealth invoked together — Kubera, who guards the treasure, and Lakshmi, who confers it — joined in a single 10-verse prayer for the fullest measure of abundance. कुबेर लक्ष्मी स्तोत्रम् calls on the Padma Nidhi and Shankha Nidhi by name and asks for gold, silver, grain, and precious gems, while working to clear away poverty, debts, and financial distress. The pairing is deliberate: guardian and giver addressed at once. It comes from the traditional wealth-worship literature and takes about 8 minutes to recite. Fridays and Thursdays suit it, with Dhanteras and Diwali its high days — a natural choice when the household wants both treasures invoked side by side.
kubera-sahasranama: A thousand names of the treasurer-god, naming Kubera as Vaishravana, as Dhanada the wealth-giver, as Navanidhisha, lord of the nine treasures. कुबेर सहस्रनाम स्तोत्रम् sets all of this into 8 verses, about 6 minutes, with particular attention to the nava nidhi he commands. Reciting it is said to draw wealth and prosperity while breaking the grip of debt and poverty. The Shiva Purana and the Yaksha tradition stand behind it, and Lakshmi and Shiva are honored alongside Kubera. It belongs to Fridays and Tuesdays, and devotees lean on it at Dhanteras and Diwali — well suited to business owners and any household working toward financial growth.
kubera-stotram: When debts pile up and prosperity will not settle, devotees turn to Kubera, treasurer of the gods, and to this hymn in his honor. कुबेर स्तोत्रम् invokes his blessing for wealth and financial abundance, asks for freedom from debts and difficulties, and prays above all for prosperity that stays — stable and lasting in the household rather than fleeting. It names both the Shankhanidhi and the Padmanidhi among the treasures it draws down. From the Vishnu Dharmottara Purana, honoring Lakshmi at Kubera's side, its 8 verses run about 8 minutes. Thursdays and Fridays carry it, and on Dhanteras and Diwali its power is felt most strongly.
kumara-ashtakam: Kumara is the boy who slew Tarakasura, the demon no grown god could defeat — and this eight-verse hymn calls on that same warrior force for the reciter's own enemies and obstacles. कुमार अष्टकम् names Kartikeya across his many forms — Skanda, Guha, Shanmukha — and asks for the threefold gift of jnana, knowledge; vairagya, dispassion; and aishwarya, sovereignty, while carrying the essence of Shadakshari mantra worship within it. Through their son, the blessings of both Shiva and Parvati reach the devotee. The recitation runs about 5 minutes. It belongs to Tuesdays, and to Skanda Shashti and Kartik Purnima — courage, victory, and clear sight invoked in equal measure.
